stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Car-share programs offer vehicles for shared, short-term use.
  • In station-based car sharing, members reserve a car at a designated lot and return it to the same lot.
  • Station-based programs are predictable and easy to manage but need dedicated lots and capital investment.
  • In free-floating car sharing, members find a car anywhere within a zone and leave it on the street within the zone.
  • Free-floating programs are flexible and inexpensive to launch but can lead to vehicles clustering inconveniently or being parked in problematic spots.
stem: The student wants to compare some disadvantages of station-based and free-floating car-share programs. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Free-floating programs can be disorganized; station-based programs, on the other hand, offer predictability and ease of management.
Station-based programs are more resource-intensive than free-floating programs, but they avoid some of the latter's clustering and parking issues.
Worth noting is that while free-floating programs are relatively easy and inexpensive to launch, they are less predictable than station-based programs.
Though free-floating programs offer flexibility, station-based car-share programs offer vehicles for shared use.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer because it pairs a disadvantage of station-based programs (more resource-intensive) with a disadvantage of free-floating programs (clustering and parking issues), as the prompt requests.

Choice A focuses on disadvantages of free-floating only. Choice C focuses on advantages of free-floating. Choice D doesn't compare disadvantages.
